name: Hydrogen
fun_fact: Hydrogen makes up 75% of all normal matter in the universe by mass.

name: Helium
fun_fact: Helium was discovered on the sun via spectroscopy in 1868 — 27 years before it was found on Earth.
found_in: natural gas deposits, alpha particle decay, sun

name: Lithium
fun_fact: Lithium-ion batteries power nearly all modern portable electronics; demand is expected to increase 40× by 2040.

name: Beryllium
fun_fact: Beryllium mirrors on the James Webb Space Telescope allow it to peer at galaxies formed just after the Big Bang.

name: Boron
fun_fact: Cubic boron nitride is the second-hardest material known — surpassed only by diamond.
found_in: borax deposits, boric acid springs, Turkey (world largest reserves)

name: Carbon
fun_fact: Graphene — a single carbon atom layer — is 200× stronger than steel and conducts electricity better than copper.
found_in: all living matter, coal, oil, natural gas, limestone

name: Nitrogen
fun_fact: The Haber-Bosch process fixes nitrogen into ammonia for fertilizers, feeding roughly half the world's population.

name: Oxygen
fun_fact: Almost all of Earth's atmospheric oxygen was produced by photosynthetic organisms over 2.4 billion years ago in the Great Oxidation Event.
found_in: atmosphere, water, Earth's crust (most abundant element)

name: Fluorine
fun_fact: Fluorine is so reactive it can make xenon — a noble gas — form chemical compounds.

name: Neon
properties: glows bright red-orange in discharge tubes
fun_fact: Neon signs actually only produce red-orange light; other colors in signs use different gases like argon or krypton.

name: Sodium
fun_fact: Nerve impulses rely entirely on sodium ion movement across cell membranes — without it no thought or movement is possible.

name: Magnesium
fun_fact: Magnesium sits at the centre of every chlorophyll molecule — without it, no photosynthesis and no plant life.

name: Aluminum
properties: most abundant metal in Earth's crust
fun_fact: Recycling aluminum uses only 5% of the energy needed to produce it from bauxite — one can save enough energy to run a TV for 3 hours.

name: Silicon
properties: second most abundant element in Earth's crust
fun_fact: A single modern CPU contains over 50 billion silicon transistors, each smaller than a coronavirus particle.
found_in: quartz, sand, granite, clay (in most rocks)

name: Phosphorus
fun_fact: Phosphorus was discovered in 1669 by alchemist Hennig Brand, who extracted it from 60 buckets of urine — the first element found by a known scientist.

name: Sulfur
fun_fact: Sulfuric acid is the most produced industrial chemical in the world — its output volume is used as an indicator of a nation's industrial strength.
found_in: volcanic deposits, pyrite, gypsum, natural gas

name: Chlorine
fun_fact: Chlorine-based drinking water disinfection is credited with saving more human lives than any other public health intervention in history.

name: Argon
properties: most abundant noble gas in atmosphere
third most abundant gas in air
fun_fact: Argon makes up 0.93% of Earth's atmosphere — nearly all produced by radioactive decay of potassium-40 in rocks over billions of years.

name: Potassium
fun_fact: White beans and dark leafy greens contain far more potassium per serving than bananas, despite the common misconception.
found_in: sylvite, carnallite, seawater, all living cells

name: Calcium
properties: most abundant metal in human body
fun_fact: 99% of the calcium in your body is in bones and teeth; the remaining 1% is critical for muscle contraction and nerve signaling.

name: Scandium
uses: aluminum alloys for aircraft and sports equipment
fun_fact: Scandium-aluminum alloys are used in Russian MiG fighter jets and professional baseball bats for extraordinary strength-to-weight ratio.
found_in: trace in many minerals, thortveitite, Scandinavian rare earth deposits

name: Titanium
properties: strongest structural metal per unit weight
fun_fact: Titanium is so biocompatible that bone cells grow directly onto it — making it ideal for dental implants and artificial joints.
found_in: ilmenite, rutile, titanite, ubiquitous in rocks

name: Vanadium
fun_fact: Vanadium flow batteries are emerging as the leading technology for grid-scale renewable energy storage.

name: Chromium
fun_fact: Chromium gives rubies their red color and emeralds their green — the same element, different crystal structures, totally different colors.

name: Manganese
fun_fact: Polymetallic nodules on the ocean floor contain vast manganese deposits — estimated to exceed all land reserves combined.

name: Iron
properties: most common element on Earth by mass
fun_fact: Earth's inner core is a solid iron-nickel crystal the size of the Moon, spinning slightly faster than the rest of the planet.

name: Cobalt
fun_fact: Cobalt-60 'gamma knife' surgery treats brain tumors with pinpoint radiation without ever cutting the skull.
found_in: cobaltite, erythrite, byproduct of nickel mining

name: Nickel
fun_fact: Some nickel-tolerant bacteria are being studied to bioremediate nickel-contaminated industrial sites.
found_in: pentlandite, garnierite, Earth's core and mantle

name: Copper
fun_fact: Copper kills bacteria on contact within hours — hospitals are replacing steel door handles with copper to reduce hospital-acquired infections.

name: Zinc
fun_fact: Zinc deficiency affects around 2 billion people worldwide and is a leading cause of preventable disease burden in developing countries.

name: Gallium
fun_fact: Gallium nitride LEDs improved lighting efficiency from 5% (incandescent) to 50%+ — the inventors won the 2014 Nobel Prize in Physics.

name: Germanium
fun_fact: Mendeleev predicted germanium's existence in 1871 with near-perfect accuracy — 15 years before its discovery — validating the periodic table.

name: Arsenic
fun_fact: Arsenic trioxide (As₂O₃) is an FDA-approved chemotherapy drug that cures a once-fatal blood cancer — one of history's oldest poisons is now a medicine.
found_in: arsenopyrite, realgar, orpiment, drinking water contamination

name: Selenium
fun_fact: Selenium deficiency causes Keshan disease (a heart condition) while excess selenium causes hair loss and a garlic-like body odor.

name: Bromine
properties: only liquid non-metallic element at room temperature
fun_fact: Dead Sea water contains up to 0.4% bromine — 50× more than regular seawater — and is mined as a major commercial source.

name: Krypton
fun_fact: From 1960–1983 the official international meter was defined by the wavelength of krypton-86 light — chosen for its remarkable precision.

name: Rubidium
fun_fact: Rubidium atomic clocks are accurate to within 1 second in 300 years and are used in GPS satellites to synchronize timing.

name: Strontium
fun_fact: Strontium-90 from nuclear fallout mimics calcium in bones; its dangers were a key driver of the 1963 Partial Nuclear Test Ban Treaty.

name: Yttrium
uses: red phosphor in TVs and LEDs
YAG lasers for surgery and industry
fun_fact: Yttrium-aluminum-garnet (YAG) lasers are used in laser eye surgery, tattoo removal, and precision metal cutting.

name: Zirconium
fun_fact: Zirconium is transparent to neutrons — ideal for wrapping nuclear fuel rods, letting reactions proceed while containing the fuel.

name: Niobium
fun_fact: Brazil controls over 90% of the world's niobium supply; without it modern skyscrapers and pipelines couldn't achieve their strength-to-weight ratios.

name: Molybdenum
fun_fact: Molybdenum is essential to nitrogenase, the enzyme that fixes atmospheric nitrogen into ammonia — critical for all biological nitrogen fixation.

name: Technetium
fun_fact: Technetium-99m is the most widely used medical radioisotope in the world — 40 million diagnostic scans per year rely on it.
found_in: uranium ores (trace), produced in nuclear reactors

name: Ruthenium
fun_fact: Ruthenium-based dye-sensitized solar cells mimic photosynthesis — potentially cheaper than silicon solar panels.

name: Rhodium
fun_fact: Rhodium is the world's most expensive metal — rarer than platinum or gold, one troy ounce can cost more than a used car.
found_in: platinum ores, byproduct of nickel/copper mining

name: Palladium
properties: absorbs up to 900× its volume in hydrogen
fun_fact: Palladium can absorb 900× its own volume of hydrogen gas — researchers are studying it as a hydrogen storage medium for fuel cells.
found_in: platinum ores, nickel-copper deposits, Russia and South Africa

name: Silver
properties: highest electrical conductivity of any element
fun_fact: Silver nanoparticles are woven into hospital fabrics and bandages to prevent bacterial infection without antibiotics.

name: Cadmium
fun_fact: The Itai-itai disease ('it hurts') in 1950s Japan was caused by cadmium-contaminated irrigation water — one of history's first documented industrial pollution disasters.

name: Indium
fun_fact: Nearly every smartphone and tablet screen contains indium tin oxide (ITO) — there may not be enough indium to sustain current production trends.

name: Tin
fun_fact: Below 13°C, white tin slowly transforms into powdery grey tin — 'tin pest' may have contributed to the failure of Napoleon's Russian campaign via disintegrating coat buttons.
found_in: cassiterite (SnO₂), tin mines in China, Malaysia, Bolivia

name: Antimony
fun_fact: Ancient Egyptians used antimony sulfide as black kohl eyeliner — modern analysis shows it may have had antibacterial properties against Nile infections.

name: Tellurium
fun_fact: Cadmium telluride solar panels are currently the cheapest form of solar electricity per watt for utility-scale installations.

name: Iodine
fun_fact: Iodine deficiency is the world's leading cause of preventable brain damage — iodizing salt has been called one of history's greatest public health victories.
found_in: seawater, kelp, thyroid glands of animals, Chilean nitrate deposits

name: Xenon
fun_fact: Xenon ion engines on NASA's Dawn spacecraft accelerated xenon atoms to 90,000 mph — providing thrust in the vacuum of space for years.

name: Cesium
fun_fact: The SI second is defined by 9,192,631,770 oscillations of cesium-133 atoms — every GPS and internet protocol depends on this definition.

name: Barium
properties: highly reactive with air and water
fun_fact: Drinking a thick barium sulfate 'milkshake' before an X-ray coats the digestive tract, making soft tissue visible — barium is dense enough to block X-rays.

name: Lanthanum
fun_fact: Each Toyota Prius battery pack contains roughly 10–15 kg of lanthanum from Chinese rare earth mines.

name: Cerium
fun_fact: Cigarette lighter flints are made from ferrocerium — an alloy of cerium and iron — not real flint. The sparks reach 3,000°F.
found_in: monazite, bastnäsite, most abundant rare earth in Earth's crust

name: Praseodymium
fun_fact: Praseodymium-doped fiber amplifiers (PDFA) boost optical signals in undersea cables, enabling high-speed internet across entire ocean basins.

name: Neodymium
uses: NdFeB permanent magnets for motors and speakers
fun_fact: Neodymium magnets are 10× stronger than regular magnets — a coin-sized one can lift 1000× its own weight, enabling miniaturized electric motors.

name: Promethium
fun_fact: Promethium is one of only two naturally radioactive elements not found on Earth — it exists only as a nuclear reactor fission product.
found_in: uranium fission products in nuclear reactors only

name: Samarium
properties: SmCo magnets stable at high temperature
uses: SmCo permanent magnets for jet engines
fun_fact: Samarium-cobalt magnets work at temperatures up to 300°C — essential for jet engine sensors where neodymium magnets would demagnetize.

name: Europium
properties: softest and most reactive rare earth
uses: red and blue phosphors in LED screens
fun_fact: Euro banknotes fluoresce under UV light due to europium-doped ink — making basic counterfeiting virtually impossible.

name: Gadolinium
properties: highest neutron capture cross-section of stable elements
fun_fact: Gadolinium-based contrast agents injected before MRI scans make soft tissues appear 10× brighter — used in ~30 million MRI procedures annually.

name: Terbium
fun_fact: Terfenol-D (terbium-dysprosium-iron) changes shape in a magnetic field — used in sonar systems and active noise cancellation in aircraft.

name: Dysprosium
properties: highest magnetic susceptibility at room temperature
uses: neodymium magnet heat-resistance additive for EVs
fun_fact: Adding 1% dysprosium to neodymium magnets allows them to operate in the 200°C+ heat of electric vehicle motors — it is a critical EV production bottleneck.

name: Holmium
properties: highest magnetic moment of any element
uses: pole pieces for the strongest electromagnets
fun_fact: Holmium lasers are used to precisely vaporize kidney stones inside the body — no surgery required.

name: Erbium
fun_fact: Erbium-doped fiber amplifiers (EDFA) are why modern internet exists — they boost light signals in undersea cables without converting to electrical signals.

name: Thulium
uses: thulium fiber lasers for metal cutting
fun_fact: Thulium-170 powered the world's first portable X-ray source — small enough to carry into remote areas without electricity.

name: Ytterbium
fun_fact: Ytterbium atomic clocks are so precise they would lose only 1 second every 10 billion years — more accurate than the age of the universe.

name: Lutetium
fun_fact: Lutetium-177 DOTATATE (Lutathera) approved in 2018 delivers radiation directly to pancreatic tumor cells via a targeting molecule.

name: Hafnium
fun_fact: Hafnium oxide replaced silicon oxide as the transistor gate dielectric in Intel's 45nm chips in 2007 — the first fundamental transistor change in 40 years.

name: Tantalum
fun_fact: About 29% of the world's tantalum comes from the Democratic Republic of Congo; the scramble for 'conflict coltan' has fueled civil wars and child labor.

name: Tungsten
properties: highest melting point of any element (3422°C)
fun_fact: Tungsten carbide is almost as hard as diamond and is used in drill bits, mining equipment, and the tiny ball-bearing tips of ballpoint pens.
found_in: wolframite, scheelite, mainly China (80% of production)

name: Rhenium
fun_fact: Single-crystal rhenium superalloy turbine blades operate above their nominal melting point — possible only through internal cooling channels and ceramic coatings.

name: Osmium
uses: osmium tetroxide for electron microscopy staining
fun_fact: Osmium tetroxide (OsO₄) is used in electron microscopy to stain lipid membranes, making the ultrastructure of cells visible at nanometer scale.
found_in: platinum group ores, Ural mountains, South Africa

name: Iridium
fun_fact: An iridium-rich layer in rocks worldwide marks the asteroid impact 66 million years ago that wiped out the dinosaurs — iridium is rare on Earth but common in asteroids.

name: Platinum
fun_fact: Cisplatin, a platinum compound discovered accidentally in 1965, has a 90%+ cure rate for testicular cancer — one of the most successful cancer treatments ever developed.
found_in: native platinum, platinum ores, South Africa (80% of supply)

name: Gold
fun_fact: All the gold ever mined by humanity would fit into a cube about 21 meters on each side — roughly 190,000 tonnes total.

name: Mercury
properties: only liquid metal at room temperature
fun_fact: Illegal artisanal gold mining releases as much mercury into the environment as all industrial sources combined — it is the world's largest mercury pollution source.

name: Thallium
properties: highly toxic — mimics potassium in cells
fun_fact: Thallium poisoning was once called 'the poisoner's poison' — its symptoms mimic many diseases, and it featured in several high-profile murder cases.
found_in: iron pyrite (trace), copper ores (trace), byproduct of zinc smelting

name: Lead
fun_fact: Some historians argue that widespread Roman lead poisoning from lead pipes and lead-lined wine vessels may have contributed to the decline of the Roman Empire.

name: Bismuth
fun_fact: Bismuth crystals grown from melt form stunning hopper-shaped spirals with iridescent rainbow oxide colors — popular in amateur crystal growing.
found_in: bismuthinite, native bismuth, byproduct of lead/copper smelting

name: Polonium
fun_fact: Just 1 microgram of polonium-210 is a lethal dose — it was used to assassinate former Russian spy Alexander Litvinenko in London in 2006.
found_in: uranium ores (trace), nuclear reactors, cigarette smoke (trace)

name: Astatine
fun_fact: The entire Earth's crust contains only about 28 grams of astatine at any given moment — it is the rarest natural element.

name: Radon
fun_fact: Radon seeping into basements from soil and rock is the second leading cause of lung cancer after smoking — responsible for ~21,000 US deaths per year.
found_in: radium decay in granite and uranium-bearing rocks

name: Francium
fun_fact: At any given moment only 20–30 grams of francium exist naturally on Earth — it is continuously created and destroyed by radioactive decay.
found_in: uranium/thorium ore (decay product), made in particle accelerators

name: Radium
fun_fact: Radium Girls — young women who painted watch dials with radium paint in the 1920s, told to lick their brushes to a point — suffered fatal radiation poisoning, sparking the first occupational safety laws.

name: Actinium
fun_fact: Actinium-225 is being investigated as a targeted alpha therapy — it can kill individual cancer cells from within after being attached to targeting antibodies.

name: Thorium
fun_fact: India is developing thorium molten salt reactors — thorium is 3× more abundant than uranium, produces less long-lived waste, and is far harder to weaponize.

name: Protactinium
fun_fact: Less than 500 kg of protactinium have ever been extracted; its primary use is tracing ancient ocean circulation patterns in paleoceanography.

name: Uranium
properties: fissile U-235 is 0.7% of natural uranium
fun_fact: Natural background radiation from uranium in granite means living in a granite building exposes you to more radiation than working in a nuclear power plant.

name: Neptunium
uses: production of Pu-238 for spacecraft power
fun_fact: Neptunium-237 is transmuted to plutonium-238 in reactors — Pu-238 powers NASA's deep space probes including both Voyager spacecraft still traveling through interstellar space.

name: Plutonium
properties: warms to the touch from its own decay
fun_fact: Pu-238 has powered 27 NASA spacecraft since 1961; the Voyager probes launched in 1977 are still generating electricity from plutonium decay in interstellar space.
found_in: uranium ores (trace), produced in nuclear reactors

name: Americium
fun_fact: Every household smoke detector contains 0.9 micrograms of Americium-241 — it ionizes air to detect smoke particles, saving tens of thousands of lives annually.

name: Curium
properties: named after Marie and Pierre Curie
fun_fact: Curium-244 powered the Alpha Proton X-ray Spectrometer on the Mars Pathfinder, Spirit, and Opportunity rovers, analyzing the chemical composition of Martian rocks.

name: Berkelium
uses: target material for producing heavier elements
fun_fact: Berkelium-249 is bombarded with calcium-48 ions to create element 117 (Tennessine) — superheavy element discovery depends on berkelium as a target.

name: Californium
properties: strongest neutron emitter of any known element
uses: neutron source for oil well logging
fun_fact: Californium-252 costs ~$27 million per gram — only 8 grams are produced annually worldwide. It is used to start nuclear reactors and treat inoperable tumors.
found_in: nuclear reactors and particle accelerators only

name: Einsteinium
fun_fact: Einsteinium was discovered in debris from the first hydrogen bomb test (Ivy Mike, 1952) by analyzing Pacific Ocean fallout — the discovery was classified for years.

name: Fermium
fun_fact: Fermium was also discovered in thermonuclear bomb test debris from Ivy Mike in 1952 — the discovery was classified during the Cold War arms race.
found_in: nuclear reactors and particle accelerators only

name: Mendelevium
fun_fact: Mendelevium was first created in 1955 by producing just 17 atoms — scientists had to chemically identify each individual atom to confirm the new element's existence.

name: Nobelium
fun_fact: Nobelium was the subject of a Cold War scientific dispute — both Soviet and American teams claimed discovery; the Soviets were ultimately credited by IUPAC in 1992.

name: Lawrencium
fun_fact: A 2015 ionization energy measurement of lawrencium at RIKEN (Japan) confirmed predictions about relativistic electron effects in superheavy elements.

name: Rutherfordium
fun_fact: Rutherfordium's chemistry has been confirmed to resemble zirconium and hafnium in group 4 — periodic table trends extend even to superheavy synthetic elements.

name: Dubnium
fun_fact: Dubnium was at the center of a Cold War naming dispute between the US (Hahnium) and Soviet scientists; IUPAC settled the controversy in 1997.

name: Seaborgium
fun_fact: Glenn Seaborg was the only person to have an element named after him while still alive — he co-discovered 10 elements and won the 1951 Nobel Prize in Chemistry.

name: Bohrium
fun_fact: Bohrium-270 has a half-life of ~61 seconds — long enough for researchers to perform limited chemical tests confirming it behaves like rhenium in group 7.

name: Hassium
fun_fact: In 2002 hassium was shown to form HsO₄ behaving similarly to osmium tetroxide — confirming group 8 periodic trends extend to superheavy elements.

name: Meitnerium
fun_fact: Meitnerium honors Lise Meitner, who co-discovered nuclear fission but was controversially excluded from the 1944 Nobel Prize — widely considered one of history's greatest scientific injustices.

name: Darmstadtium
fun_fact: Darmstadtium was first created at GSI Helmholtz Centre in 1994 using a nickel-64 beam hitting lead-208 — just 3 atoms were produced in the initial experiment.

name: Roentgenium
fun_fact: Relativistic electron effects may make roentgenium chemically resemble gold — theoretical predictions suggest it should be more noble than expected from group 11.

name: Copernicium
fun_fact: Relativistic calculations predict copernicium may be a gas at room temperature — behaving like a noble gas rather than a metal, defying all group 12 trends.

name: Nihonium
fun_fact: Nihonium was discovered by the RIKEN team in Japan after 9 years of experiments — the first element named by an Asian scientific team, after Nihon (Japan).

name: Flerovium
fun_fact: Flerovium is predicted to be a volatile solid or gas due to relativistic effects — it may behave nothing like lead despite being in the same group.

name: Moscovium
fun_fact: Moscovium synthesized at JINR in Dubna decays to nihonium — tracing these decay chains maps out the predicted 'island of stability' for superheavy nuclei.

name: Livermorium
fun_fact: Livermorium was created in a US–Russian collaboration between Lawrence Livermore and JINR Dubna — a scientific partnership that has produced several new elements.

name: Tennessine
properties: would be heaviest halogen if stable
fun_fact: Tennessine was first synthesized in 2010, named for Tennessee in honor of Oak Ridge National Laboratory, Vanderbilt University, and the University of Tennessee.

name: Oganesson
properties: predicted to be a solid despite noble gas position
fun_fact: Despite sitting in the noble gas group, relativistic quantum calculations predict oganesson should be a solid at room temperature — the ultimate breakdown of periodic table trends. Only 3–4 atoms have ever been made.
